speaker: interviewer
text: Three interview with Mr. Zalman Finkelstein, August 15th, 1996. Who went to Auschwitz with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my sister that is older from me two years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Gershon is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a brother that he was two or three years younger. I don't know. Hershl and me-- five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you together in the car-- in the train-- all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you were ever heard of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw in the beginning-- when we-- the way this was handled-- took us on trucks, not prepare the people to walk up and sit down. Like they said, you are going to work. And-- and then we got to do the-- where the cargo was there-- the-- the-- the-- the train or whatever, and this is-- the openings of those trains is very high-- the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's this high. All people-- woman, the heavy people-- couldn't walk up, couldn't jump up to here and then pull yourself in. And at the same time, you had the whole length-- I don't know how many-- this-- what do you call this? Each segment-- each-- each--

speaker: interviewer
text: The cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: This cars-- those cars, when you didn't see an end of it-- and everybody-- old women, people-- they cannot go in-- nothing to walk on to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get in?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're screaming. And the Germans screaming the whole length-- you [GERMAN], you-- you-- you [GERMAN] with this, with that, you know, all kind of this. They make you right away low, nothing. You're not worth anything. So what happened was that we had to pull everybody up and help each other. And they filled it up. They didn't-- in those cargo-- they filled it up with I don't know how many people. You were sitting, like-- packed each other close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and then they locked it-- locked it, and it was just an opening there with a little screen on top in both ends. And I'll tell you something. Excuse me, you were making a [INAUDIBLE] in this-- in this place, peeing in the place on each other. You turned in not a human anymore. That's it. You saw you were going for something terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you [BACKROUND NOISE] take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. What kind of clothes did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was wearing. I don't know. I don't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- this was 1943? Yeah, 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were, what, about 16, 17?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I mean, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: About?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, you were sitting inside in the dark, and nothing-- a little light on top there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the train stop at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- they stop once in a few times. So one time, they throw in a herring-- herring. We didn't have for days a little water to drink, and they give you herrings to eat-- schmaltz herring, you know, salty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many died? A fortune of people died in the-- in those-- in those trains. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Auschwitz, in the daytime or at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I saw that it's a camp with the-- with the-- like the ghetto-- the walls--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing you saw when you got out-- out of the train? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible thing-- terrible. What happened is they opened up those doors, sliding doors-- both sides-- one here, one there. And they start screaming-- this was not Germans, again, Jewish. And they were working over there-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I-- thought it's Germans. I thought it German-- [GERMAN]!

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]! Old women-- my mother was-- was-- was-- was hardly move. And this what happened-- everybody down who couldn't walk, who was dead-- they left, and they cleaned it up. And-- and everything and over there where the train stopped was asphalt. And everybody stood-- fives, tens, you know, but they were packed together along-- the way the train-- this one. I thought all the time that those people that they say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I learned later-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal clothes with the-- the [INAUDIBLE], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in the striped uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are Germans there too, in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Germans stood on both sides--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with dogs--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --German Shepherds, with other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the line-- and in the front of the line was the officers-- big shots-- and they select, this side, this side, this side, this side. And my mother says to me, Zalman, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Oh, forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. That's OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Means that you should stand tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bigger, bigger, bigger, to show that I'm not 13 years old-- nothing to do with you and this saved me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were with your sister or separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, no, we together down, and the sister went one way, I went another way. And my mother with the two kids-- she went in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know where we should go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going into one of the barracks-- not right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go? What was the process?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we stood in an area there. And over there was people that watch out. And then your stood in line, and they sheared the hair. They took the hair off. And they-- and they-- and they-- they shaved you down here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this shaving felt [? murder. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that-- I don't know what they had-- blades no blades. And the guy grabbed you like-- like you were a wild man, and you cut and bleeded and-- and this. And this is not [? chop. ?] One after another he does this, you know. They didn't care, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did it, Jews or--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jew. Did they speak to you when they were doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you have to do it. But as a human being, behave nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did speak to you at all when they were doing-- shaving hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knock your teeth out. You were afraid. You cried, you this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they put you in to take a shower. So packed a-- double or triple the amount that can-- they could shower. And they put it in, and they put hot boiled water-- you can make the hardest stuff soft-- boiled water. So you start running to the walls. Here, the two guys going around in those wooden shoes with a stick-- a wooden stick. And he's over there-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] you know, you're dirty, you're filthy, and this and that. You don't want to wash. [SPEAKING GERMAN], ice cold. You couldn't go in there-- ice cold, ice! You know, so this is what we went through, and then they put us in-- in a-- in a barrack, filled up the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet, naked. Now you go into the barrack, and a guy comes, you know, and the-- those barracks in Auschwitz used to be a Polish army camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What barrack were you in? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five or six, I don't know. Oh, I think one or two. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who-- who was remembering a number? So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so this was-- they used to keep horses in it. So you had the place where they used to eat the horses-- in both sides, horses, you know? And this place-- they used to fill it up with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did now, they put a piece of wood on top, and they closed it. And on both sides, people were there. And we were sitting this close-- each other close-- all the way till this. So many people they packed in. We were sitting and waiting. Then comes in a guy. He goes on top of this. They're sticking their hands, you know, to put it up here. And he holds it, and he said, who wants to live? Who wants to live? You better give me the gold. Give me the-- the diamonds, what you hide it, you swallow it, whatever. You give it because you're gonna be fried. You give the gold if you have it, and tell me where it is. Some people did. Did you have gold?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He-- he speaks generally to everybody. But some people had. After he passed the whole thing, I knew there were guys that had gold inside they swallowed. Swallowed-- they-- one guy had two diamonds-- nice big ones-- swallowed. He said, I'm gonna die anyway. Why to give them?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So and after this, we went out. They put us in-- in the blocks. They give out the clothes. But they took off before-- before they went in the crematorium-- all those clothes, you know. So they give one-- he-- he needs that long, and he gets this long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? No underwear, no nothing, no shoes-- bare feet till we got some shoes later on they gave. Then you couldn't walk it. It's too small, too-- one is this, and one is this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you saying to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd stand [? there. ?] Some people killed themselves. Some people could throw themselves on the wires of the-- of the-- of the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a number on-- you have a number on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show us the number on your arm, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who put the number on the arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the triangle stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Jew is a-- a-- a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Political?

speaker: interviewee
text: A political prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sign of a political prisoner. OK. How long did you stay in that barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Six months, seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, they-- they put out to clean the areas, digging for people that coming out from the crematorium-- holes, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Digging holes to bury them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To bury them.

speaker: interviewer
text: As bodies, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: As bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: As bodies, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bodies, bodies. They-- they-- then later on, you get a little know the guy that managed in-- in the group that you work. So you get-- and then I worked the cleaning a little-- it was a-- was a-- over there, they would made bricks to-- they make bricks. So I worked there a little bit cleaning, bringing sand, doing this, this with everybody had to work. But later on, I got-- the first time, I got closer to the crematoriums. And the crematoriums-- the guys that were working-- they-- they couldn't-- nobody could talk to them because this was an isolated area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what did the guys do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other guys? Oh, the guys that shoveled in the people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is what they do-- they put them on this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are those--

speaker: interviewee
text: --this ledge. And then the sliding in--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were taking the bodies out-- out of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this-- the crematorium-- they were burned there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They lit all that was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they taking the bodies from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bodies?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a mountain two stories high with dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were taking these bodies--

speaker: interviewee
text: They die, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: If then they didn't die, they poisoned them-- that's it-- to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put them in the gas chamber. They had-- the-- the-- the gas chamber was so full, they couldn't make it. So they poisoned people by drinking something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they burned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Burned them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't burn them that much. You know, what they had there before-- when I left Auschwitz, I think they had, three stories high, a mountain of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I, you know-- you wanted to go to work because you knew, if you stay in the barrack, or you're not going, you're gone. They used to pick up every day people they needed to burn. You better go to work and better not being sick. Sick people used to go till they couldn't walk anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened then is, they took me and other people to clean around the-- the gas chamber over there, not to have signs that people get the uprising before to see what's happening. But they [INAUDIBLE] at the end of the buildings, you know, no matter what-- always was filthy dirt from the people. They-- they're screaming, and they're clawing. They see they're going to dead-- to die. So this is what we used to clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you cleaned it. With what did you clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had this road-- the-- the-- the wagons on two wheels-- they did-- with one wheel. You lift it up, and the whole thing folds up, you know? So we used to be close to this. We have the ashes stay out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ashes of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ashes of people, everything. Used to fill up ashes they used to throw out. If I'm not-- they used to-- this was hidden, but they used to have-- I believe they used to have their people-- farmers-- coming in the back. There was a ditch there lower down. Over there was ashes galore. They didn't know where to put it. They tried to dig holes to dig it to cover this, but they used it for farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: As fertilizer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fertilizer.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you had the wagon with the ashes--

speaker: interviewee
text: We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hole, in-- in a ditch, all the dirt around it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you when you were doing it? How many of you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, quite a few people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. A month, month and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could choose to do something else, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I went away already. I went away to the other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, you were transferred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Transferred to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you offer to be transferred, or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They simply took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they-- they-- each time, when you stood in counting. They're counting.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Zahlappell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning. They count every morning-- who everybody's at work, the people that working that day. So this-- so they used to-- sometime every week or every two weeks-- asking for professionals-- who was a shoemaker, who was a barber, who was a everything, you know. So they came out [INAUDIBLE] [? die ?] maker So I lift up a hand, and I stood on my feet. I stood-- oh, oh, my god. I don't-- I forgot. That doesn't work. It's going to be in it. [LAUGHTER] Sorry, I almost ripped it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so I-- I-- I-- I stood up here, you know, automatic, without thinking, lifting up the hand. [POINTING] But that's a little different. This is a photographer. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, I'm smiling with my tears. So anyway, so I lifted up, and I stood on my toes to see that I'm big man, you know. I'm not a little boy. So they-- everybody-- you're out, out, out, out, out, out. And I went to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Going, and going-- I don't know, 10 days, five days. I don't know, you're--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sit packed in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Dachau, what did you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- they put you in a-- in a block.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a block. Do you know which block you were in in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the block similar to the barracks in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a block in Dachau like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- was-- it was about 50 people in-- I think 40 or 50 people in the barrack. And the barrack was built in such a way that the roof goes down into the earth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slants.

speaker: interviewee
text: And grass grows on it. Not the roof there, but it's not [INAUDIBLE] so that it's flat. It was a lot of-- this place is big. And-- and you walked in lower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? And there was wooden-- the whole length with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in a bed-- in a-- in a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was maybe 50 people in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how many in a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- did you have to sleep with a bunch of other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're laying like-- like this, you know. And just wood. There used to be a little hay on the-- on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a kapo in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, there was a kapo. Mhm. There was a blockalteste and a kapo. The blockalteste there was the guy who was responsible with this group that is in this-- in this room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was good, this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made him good?

speaker: interviewee
text: He tried to help. He didn't kick. He didn't hit. He just hit-- sometime he gave a kick, you know, but the kick is not the kick that kills you. Some would give you a kick that you couldn't sit already for weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but the kapo-- the same thing. Some were human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish too?

speaker: interviewee
text: If the Germans were stood, he make believe that he hits, that he's good because he wanted to have this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this kapo Jewish too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they all Jewish. But what they did-- their trick was too [? easy ?] the Germans. They're mixed. If it's a new guy in a group of people Jewish, they take a Polack or a Czech. And if it's a Romanian, he takes a Hungarian to be a kapo. So for some reason, this makes a little hate between if you didn't listen to him right away and you didn't do the right way. So he makes in front of the Germans that he is good. He is good, he is with them. So they liked him, so he had the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people get special privileges-- any people get special privileges?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only way if the-- mine-- mine-- this was a blockalteste there. He loved me. He liked me very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw-- he saw in me a little bit of-- I'm more a little a mensch with feelings, you know? I was talking to him when he got a little-- I felt I have no afraid anymore for him that he's going to do something to me. And he listened. And-- and then I had a friend over there. Not this one. This is after-- I had-- before-- this mine-- mine that I got with this one to do. Every morning, before you went to work, you go out in the field for the-- an appell. In German is an appell, where you stayed up. And every barrack were stood-- stood there with their-- with their blockalteste, with this guy. And he stood in the front. And this is a few thousand people like. And the German officer comes and counts if everybody is there. And each blockalteste goes after this German. He finished his line, the blockalteste stays with his part, and the German continues to another group of people, and so the other blockalteste. Now, this friend that I was friendly with him, he got sick. Every morning, when we left, nobody could-- every morning, when we went to work, the Germans checks the barracks. They used to check it if nobody hide or do something. So he got so sick, so every time, when you're stood in the line, sometime a doctor, if he was a doctor-- the German-- to look to see if nobody's sick with a disease. They were afraid because they have the army-- the soldiers-- right outside the camp by their camp. So they're afraid to have anything to do with us. So they watched. If nobody's anything [INAUDIBLE] sick, [BACKROUND NOISE] so they used to check just-- they used to tell everybody to open this, you know, to check-- oh-- to check it if he is not sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to him-- this blockalteste-- and I said, listen, he-- he cannot walk, and he is filled with [INAUDIBLE]. You know what [INAUDIBLE] is? He's filled with [INAUDIBLE]. And I don't want him to go. You know, [INAUDIBLE] with guys, a head and a foot, and [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's it-- goes in the crematorium. So but there was not crematorium, so they would put them in the-- in this-- they had ditches with the bulldozers, builded up. And the people with the wagons they used to carry the deaths out there and lay them out like herrings, you know. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, listen, I don't want him to die. So I get [INAUDIBLE]. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he didn't say anything. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And he is telling me, if he doesn't have [INAUDIBLE], [SPEAKING GERMAN], this, a nail, had something-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], that he passes. He sees it's nice and clean, you know, pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't walk. And when he passed by, he saw something's wrong. He calls right away two guys, put him on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Dachau? You volunteered as a-- you said you were a professional. What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me-- they put me in-- in a-- in a [INAUDIBLE] [? die maker. ?] Half of this place was Polacks working-- all kinds of Polacks. Half was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and they put me in the milling machine. They give me the [INAUDIBLE]. The German-- the meister-- he was limping on a foot with a stick. He was walking with a cane. When I came in, I heard screaming. I didn't know that this is here. What a [INAUDIBLE] this is. And you murdered wild men-- old men-- he didn't have teeth in the mouth. But he was a-- an [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He would eat a Jew up. And [LAUGHS] I stood this near machine. A guy brought over the iron with the-- with the little wagon, you know, little thing, puts down-- puts down the-- the-- the-- the plan of what to do and walked away. This is yours. Near me was a Jewish guy working. He was-- I don't know-- 50, 55, 52. And he-- I saw he works. Boy, oh boy. I knew to put the machine on, you know, looked a little here, there, there, there, there. When my father was working, it was an old machine. This was a better machine-- German machine-- so a milling machine. And I knew this guy is going to come. The guy is going to check it. I didn't know about this [INAUDIBLE]-- this-- this guy. So I went over to this guy, and I say, you know, help me. I don't know what to do here. I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He took out his [? ready ?] part. He put this in my machine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said, [GERMAN]-- leave the machine working, and have the cutting on the spot. You see him coming in the back, make believe you start cutting, and this, put the machine in place, you know, like this. And when he goes to you, you see him and say, [GERMAN]-- I finished your piece! You know? And this what I did. And he comes to me, and he says, finished? Yeah, finished. See? Good. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He-- in mean time, another one-- the Jewish guy. We're going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was two months like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change our tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, two months I was with this guy.
